revision.text:
{{Short description|Chinese gold seal discovered in Japan}} [[File:King of Na gold seal.jpg|thumb|upright=1.15|Composite image showing two views of the seal]] [[File:King of Na gold seal knob top.png|thumb|upright=1.15|Top view of the snake knob]]
The '''King of Na gold seal''' ({{langx|ja|漢委奴国王印}}) is a solid [[:en:Gold|Gold]] [[Seal (East Asia)|seal]] discovered in the year 1784 on [[Shikanoshima Island]] in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The seal is designated as a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure of Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book | publisher = [[Yomiuri Shimbun]] | title = National Treasures of Japan – Exhibition catalogue, April 10 - May 27, 1990, Tokyo National Museum | year = 1990 | language = Japanese }}</ref> The seal is believed to have been cast in [[:en:Han dynasty|China]] and bestowed by [[:en:Emperor Guangwu of Han|Emperor Guangwu of Han]] upon a diplomatic official (envoy) visiting from Japan in the year 57 AD. The five [[:en:Kanji|Chinese characters]] appearing on the seal identify it as the seal of the King of Na state of [[Wa (Japan)]], [[vassal state]] of the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]].<ref>{{Citation | last = Miyake | first = Yonekichi | title = 漢委奴國王印考 (A study of the seal [inscribed] to the King of the state of Na in Wa under the Han dynasty) | journal = Shigaku Zasshi | volume = 3 | issue = 37 |date=December 1892 | pages = 874–81 }}</ref> The seal is currently in the collection of the [[:en:Fukuoka City Museum|Fukuoka City Museum]] in [[:en:Fukuoka|Fukuoka]], Japan. The seal is very well known in Japan, as its taught in Japanese history books to be a cultural asset which ascertains how Japan as a political entity came into being. Its the first known textual record of Japan as a country.<ref name="CityMuseum">{{Cite web | title = The Permanent Exhibitions of the Fukuoka City Museum | publisher = Fukuoka City Museum | accessdate = 2023-05-10 | url = https://museum.city.fukuoka.jp/en/exhibition.html }}</ref>
== Appearance == The seal is composed of gold of 95% purity.<ref>{{Cite web | title = The Gold Seal, "Kan no Wa no Na no Kokuo" | publisher = Fukuoka Art Museum | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.fukuoka-art-museum.jp/english/eb/html/eb01/2011/kin_in/kin_in.html }}</ref> It is made up of a square base, with the seal itself on the bottom face, and a handle on top of the base in the shape of a coiled serpent. It has a mass of {{convert|108.729|g}}. The total height from base to handle is {{convert|2.236|cm}}. The base of the seal averages {{convert|2.347|cm}} on a side. This dimension roughly corresponds to the traditional Chinese standard unit of length of one [[:en:Cun (unit)|cun]], as used in the [[Han dynasty#Eastern Han|Later Han dynasty]] (about {{convert|2.304|cm|disp=sqbr}}).<ref>{{Cite web | title = 金印 |trans-title=Gold Seal | publisher = Fukuoka Prefectural Shakaikyouiku Sougou Center | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.fsg.pref.fukuoka.jp/bunka/contents_detail.asp?table=t27&fields=f1332&id=1307-102&bunrui=kuni_yuke_biju_koko_koku | language = Japanese }}</ref>
=== Characters engraved on the seal === {{multiple image |total_width=260 |footer=Base of seal (left), together with its imprint (right). The imprint is read from right to left, top to bottom. |width1=149|height1=145| image1 = King of Na gold seal face.png |width2=149|height2=145| image2 = King of Na gold seal imprint.svg }}
The five characters engraved on the seal are (in the order in which they are to be read): : 漢委奴國王
The meanings of these characters (in the context of this seal) are: "Han" (referring to the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]] of China), "[[Wa (Japan)|Wa]]" (an ancient name for Japan), "[[Nakoku|Na]]" (an ancient kingdom / state within Japan), "state / country", and "ruler." Altogether, the meaning of the seal inscription is: "(seal of) the King of the Na state of the Wa [vassal?] of the Han dynasty".
The character 委 is a loan for 倭 ([[Wa (Japan)|Wa]]), an instance of the common practice of [[:zh-tw:通假字|loaning characters]] in Classical Chinese.<ref>{{Cite journal |author=黃當時 |year=2011 |title=金印 「漢委奴国王」 の読みと意味について |journal=中国言語文化研究 |number=11 |publisher=佛教大学中国言語文化研究会 |url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/CB/0011/CB00110L001.pdf |access-date=2023-09-18 }}</ref> The characters are engraved in the [[seal script]] style.
== History == The seal has been judged to be the one described in the ''[[:en:Book of the Later Han|Book of the Later Han]]'', a Chinese chronicle of the history of the [[:en:Eastern Han dynasty|Eastern Han dynasty]]. According to the chronicle, the Chinese [[:en:Emperor Guangwu of Han|Emperor Guangwu]] conferred the seal on a diplomatic official visiting from Japan.
=== Contemporary description of conferral === The following is the original Chinese text from the chronicle: :建武中元二年，倭奴國奉貢朝賀，使人自稱大夫，倭國之極南界也。光武賜以印綬。<ref>{{Cite web | title = 後漢書·東夷列傳第七十五 | trans-title = Houhanshu Part 75 | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.xysa.net/a200/h350/03houhanshu/t-115.htm | language = Chinese | archive-date = 2010-03-31 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20100331133322/http://www.xysa.net/a200/h350/03houhanshu/t-115.htm | url-status = dead }}</ref>
This passage can be translated into English as: :"In the 2nd year of the ''jianwu zhongyuan'' reign period [AD 57], the [[Nakoku|Na]] state of [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] sent an envoy with tribute. The envoy introduced himself as a high official. The state lies in the [[Kyushu|far south]] of Wa. [Emperor] Guangwu bestowed on him a seal with a tassel."<ref name="CityMuseum" /><ref>{{Cite book | last = Tsunoda | first = Ryusaku | title = Japan in the Chinese Dynastic Histories: Later Han Through Ming Dynasties | editor-last = Goodrich | editor-first = L. Carrington | publisher = P.D. and Ione Perkins | location = South Pasadena, CA | year = 1951 | pages = 187 | series = Perkins Asiatic monographs }}</ref>
During the Han dynasty, similar seals were bestowed on other regional sovereigns, in an attempt by the dynasty to bring these sovereigns into the Han ruling order.<ref name="CityMuseum" />
=== Rediscovery === [[File:Kin-in Park02.jpg|thumb|260px|Commemorative monument near the site where the seal was uncovered in 1784]] After being lost for an undetermined period of time, the seal was reportedly rediscovered on April 12, 1784, on [[Shika Island]] in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], Japan. According to contemporary reports, the seal was discovered by a farmer named Jinbei while repairing an irrigation ditch. It was found surrounded by stones forming a box-like structure around it. The stone above the seal required two adults to lift. After rediscovery, the seal was kept by the [[Kuroda clan]], rulers of the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]], and eventually donated by the Kuroda family to the city of Fukuoka in 1978.<ref name="CityMuseum" />
== See also == * [[:en:King of Dian gold seal|King of Dian gold seal]] * [[:en:King of Nanyue gold seal|King of Nanyue gold seal]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)]]
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Chinese gold seal discovered in Japan}} [[File:King of Na gold seal.jpg|thumb|upright=1.15|Composite image showing two views of the seal]] [[File:King of Na gold seal knob top.png|thumb|upright=1.15|Top view of the snake knob]]
The '''King of Na gold seal''' ({{langx|ja|漢委奴国王印}}) is a solid [[:en:Gold|Gold]] [[Seal (East Asia)|seal]] discovered in the year 1784 on [[Shikanoshima Island]] in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The seal is designated as a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure of Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book | publisher = [[Yomiuri Shimbun]] | title = National Treasures of Japan – Exhibition catalogue, April 10 - May 27, 1990, Tokyo National Museum | year = 1990 | language = Japanese }}</ref> The seal is believed to have been cast in [[:en:Han dynasty|China]] and bestowed by [[:en:Emperor Guangwu of Han|Emperor Guangwu of Han]] upon a diplomatic official (envoy) visiting from Japan in the year 57 AD. The five [[:en:Kanji|Chinese characters]] appearing on the seal identify it as the seal of the King of Na state of [[Wa (Japan)]], [[vassal state]] of the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]].<ref>{{Citation | last = Miyake | first = Yonekichi | title = 漢委奴國王印考 (A study of the seal [inscribed] to the King of the state of Na in Wa under the Han dynasty) | journal = Shigaku Zasshi | volume = 3 | issue = 37 |date=December 1892 | pages = 874–81 }}</ref> The seal is currently in the collection of the [[:en:Fukuoka City Museum|Fukuoka City Museum]] in [[:en:Fukuoka|Fukuoka]], Japan. The seal is very well known in Japan, as its taught in Japanese history books to be a cultural asset which ascertains how Japan as a political entity came into being. Its the first known textual record of Japan as a country.<ref name="CityMuseum">{{Cite web | title = The Permanent Exhibitions of the Fukuoka City Museum | publisher = Fukuoka City Museum | accessdate = 2023-05-10 | url = https://museum.city.fukuoka.jp/en/exhibition.html }}</ref>
== Appearance == The seal is composed of gold of 95% purity.<ref>{{Cite web | title = The Gold Seal, "Kan no Wa no Na no Kokuo" | publisher = Fukuoka Art Museum | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.fukuoka-art-museum.jp/english/eb/html/eb01/2011/kin_in/kin_in.html }}</ref> It is made up of a square base, with the seal itself on the bottom face, and a handle on top of the base in the shape of a coiled serpent. It has a mass of {{convert|108.729|g}}. The total height from base to handle is {{convert|2.236|cm}}. The base of the seal averages {{convert|2.347|cm}} on a side. This dimension roughly corresponds to the traditional Chinese standard unit of length of one [[:en:Cun (unit)|cun]], as used in the [[Han dynasty#Eastern Han|Later Han dynasty]] (about {{convert|2.304|cm|disp=sqbr}}).<ref>{{Cite web | title = 金印 |trans-title=Gold Seal | publisher = Fukuoka Prefectural Shakaikyouiku Sougou Center | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.fsg.pref.fukuoka.jp/bunka/contents_detail.asp?table=t27&fields=f1332&id=1307-102&bunrui=kuni_yuke_biju_koko_koku | language = Japanese }}</ref>
=== Characters engraved on the seal === {{multiple image |total_width=260 |footer=Base of seal (left), together with its imprint (right). The imprint is read from right to left, top to bottom. |width1=149|height1=145| image1 = King of Na gold seal face.png |width2=149|height2=145| image2 = King of Na gold seal imprint.svg }}
The five characters engraved on the seal are (in the order in which they are to be read): : 漢委奴國王
The meanings of these characters (in the context of this seal) are: "Han" (referring to the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]] of China), "[[Wa (Japan)|Wa]]" (an ancient name for Japan), "[[Nakoku|Na]]" (an ancient kingdom / state within Japan), "state / country", and "ruler." Altogether, the meaning of the seal inscription is: "(seal of) the King of the Na state of the Wa [vassal?] of the Han dynasty".
The character 委 is a loan for 倭 ([[Wa (Japan)|Wa]]), an instance of the common practice of [[:zh-tw:通假字|loaning characters]] in Classical Chinese.<ref>{{Cite journal |author=黃當時 |year=2011 |title=金印 「漢委奴国王」 の読みと意味について |journal=中国言語文化研究 |number=11 |publisher=佛教大学中国言語文化研究会 |url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/CB/0011/CB00110L001.pdf |access-date=2023-09-18 }}</ref> The characters are engraved in the [[seal script]] style.
== History == The seal has been judged to be the one described in the ''[[:en:Book of the Later Han|Book of the Later Han]]'', a Chinese chronicle of the history of the [[:en:Eastern Han dynasty|Eastern Han dynasty]]. According to the chronicle, the Chinese [[:en:Emperor Guangwu of Han|Emperor Guangwu]] conferred the seal on a diplomatic official visiting from Japan.
=== Contemporary description of conferral === The following is the original Chinese text from the chronicle: :建武中元二年，倭奴國奉貢朝賀，使人自稱大夫，倭國之極南界也。光武賜以印綬。<ref>{{Cite web | title = 後漢書·東夷列傳第七十五 | trans-title = Houhanshu Part 75 | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.xysa.net/a200/h350/03houhanshu/t-115.htm | language = Chinese | archive-date = 2010-03-31 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20100331133322/http://www.xysa.net/a200/h350/03houhanshu/t-115.htm | url-status = dead }}</ref>
This passage can be translated into English as: :"In the 2nd year of the ''jianwu zhongyuan'' reign period [AD 57], the [[Nakoku|Na]] state of [[Wa (Japan)|Wa]] sent an envoy with tribute. The envoy introduced himself as a high official. The state lies in the [[Kyushu|far south]] of Wa. [Emperor] Guangwu bestowed on him a seal with a tassel."<ref name="CityMuseum" /><ref>{{Cite book | last = Tsunoda | first = Ryusaku | title = Japan in the Chinese Dynastic Histories: Later Han Through Ming Dynasties | editor-last = Goodrich | editor-first = L. Carrington | publisher = P.D. and Ione Perkins | location = South Pasadena, CA | year = 1951 | pages = 187 | series = Perkins Asiatic monographs }}</ref>
During the Han dynasty, similar seals were bestowed on other regional sovereigns, in an attempt by the dynasty to bring these sovereigns into the Han ruling order.<ref name="CityMuseum" />
=== Rediscovery === [[File:Kin-in Park02.jpg|thumb|260px|Commemorative monument near the site where the seal was uncovered in 1784]] After being lost for an undetermined period of time, the seal was reportedly rediscovered on April 12, 1784, on [[Shika Island]] in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], Japan. According to contemporary reports, the seal was discovered by a farmer named Jinbei while repairing an irrigation ditch. It was found surrounded by stones forming a box-like structure around it. The stone above the seal required two adults to lift. After rediscovery, the seal was kept by the [[:en:Kuroda clan|Kuroda clan]], rulers of the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]], and eventually donated by the Kuroda family to the city of Fukuoka in 1978.<ref name="CityMuseum" />
== See also == * [[:en:King of Dian gold seal|King of Dian gold seal]] * [[:en:King of Nanyue gold seal|King of Nanyue gold seal]] * [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)|List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)]]
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

}}

{{Short description|Chinese gold seal discovered in Japan}} [[File:King of Na gold seal.jpg|thumb|upright=1.15|Composite image showing two views of the seal]] [[File:King of Na gold seal knob top.png|thumb|upright=1.15|Top view of the snake knob]]
The '''King of Na gold seal''' ({{langx|ja|漢委奴国王印}}) is a solid [[:en:Gold|Gold]] [[Seal (East Asia)|seal]] discovered in the year 1784 on [[Shikanoshima Island]] in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The seal is designated as a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book | publisher = [[Yomiuri Shimbun]] | title = National Treasures of Japan – Exhibition catalogue, April 10 - May 27, 1990, Tokyo National Museum | year = 1990 | language = Japanese }}</ref> The seal is believed to have been cast in [[:en:Han dynasty|China]] and bestowed by [[:en:Emperor Guangwu of Han|Emperor Guangwu of Han]] upon a diplomatic official (envoy) visiting from Japan in the year 57 AD. The five [[:en:Kanji|Chinese characters]] appearing on the seal identify it as the seal of the King of Na state of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa (Japan)]], [[vassal state]] of the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]].<ref>{{Citation | last = Miyake | first = Yonekichi | title = 漢委奴國王印考 (A study of the seal [inscribed] to the King of the state of Na in Wa under the Han dynasty) | journal = Shigaku Zasshi | volume = 3 | issue = 37 |date=December 1892 | pages = 874–81 }}</ref> The seal is currently in the collection of the [[:en:Fukuoka City Museum|Fukuoka City Museum]] in [[:en:Fukuoka|Fukuoka]], Japan. The seal is very well known in Japan, as its taught in Japanese history books to be a cultural asset which ascertains how Japan as a political entity came into being. Its the first known textual record of Japan as a country.<ref name="CityMuseum">{{Cite web | title = The Permanent Exhibitions of the Fukuoka City Museum | publisher = Fukuoka City Museum | accessdate = 2023-05-10 | url = https://museum.city.fukuoka.jp/en/exhibition.html }}</ref>
== Appearance == The seal is composed of gold of 95% purity.<ref>{{Cite web | title = The Gold Seal, "Kan no Wa no Na no Kokuo" | publisher = Fukuoka Art Museum | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.fukuoka-art-museum.jp/english/eb/html/eb01/2011/kin_in/kin_in.html }}</ref> It is made up of a square base, with the seal itself on the bottom face, and a handle on top of the base in the shape of a coiled serpent. It has a mass of {{convert|108.729|g}}. The total height from base to handle is {{convert|2.236|cm}}. The base of the seal averages {{convert|2.347|cm}} on a side. This dimension roughly corresponds to the traditional Chinese standard unit of length of one [[:en:Cun (unit)|cun]], as used in the [[Han dynasty#Eastern Han|Later Han dynasty]] (about {{convert|2.304|cm|disp=sqbr}}).<ref>{{Cite web | title = 金印 |trans-title=Gold Seal | publisher = Fukuoka Prefectural Shakaikyouiku Sougou Center | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.fsg.pref.fukuoka.jp/bunka/contents_detail.asp?table=t27&fields=f1332&id=1307-102&bunrui=kuni_yuke_biju_koko_koku | language = Japanese }}</ref>
=== Characters engraved on the seal === {{multiple image |total_width=260 |footer=Base of seal (left), together with its imprint (right). The imprint is read from right to left, top to bottom. |width1=149|height1=145| image1 = King of Na gold seal face.png |width2=149|height2=145| image2 = King of Na gold seal imprint.svg }}
The five characters engraved on the seal are (in the order in which they are to be read): : 漢委奴國王
The meanings of these characters (in the context of this seal) are: "Han" (referring to the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]] of China), "[[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]" (an ancient name for Japan), "[[:en:Nakoku|Na]]" (an ancient kingdom / state within Japan), "state / country", and "ruler." Altogether, the meaning of the seal inscription is: "(seal of) the King of the Na state of the Wa [vassal?] of the Han dynasty".
The character 委 is a loan for 倭 ([[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]), an instance of the common practice of [[:zh-tw:通假字|loaning characters]] in Classical Chinese.<ref>{{Cite journal |author=黃當時 |year=2011 |title=金印 「漢委奴国王」 の読みと意味について |journal=中国言語文化研究 |number=11 |publisher=佛教大学中国言語文化研究会 |url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/CB/0011/CB00110L001.pdf |access-date=2023-09-18 }}</ref> The characters are engraved in the [[seal script]] style.
== History == The seal has been judged to be the one described in the ''[[:en:Book of the Later Han|Book of the Later Han]]'', a Chinese chronicle of the history of the [[:en:Eastern Han dynasty|Eastern Han dynasty]]. According to the chronicle, the Chinese [[:en:Emperor Guangwu of Han|Emperor Guangwu]] conferred the seal on a diplomatic official visiting from Japan.
=== Contemporary description of conferral === The following is the original Chinese text from the chronicle: :建武中元二年，倭奴國奉貢朝賀，使人自稱大夫，倭國之極南界也。光武賜以印綬。<ref>{{Cite web | title = 後漢書·東夷列傳第七十五 | trans-title = Houhanshu Part 75 | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.xysa.net/a200/h350/03houhanshu/t-115.htm | language = Chinese | archive-date = 2010-03-31 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20100331133322/http://www.xysa.net/a200/h350/03houhanshu/t-115.htm | url-status = dead }}</ref>
This passage can be translated into English as: :"In the 2nd year of the ''jianwu zhongyuan'' reign period [AD 57], the [[:en:Nakoku|Na]] state of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] sent an envoy with tribute. The envoy introduced himself as a high official. The state lies in the [[Kyushu|far south]] of Wa. [Emperor] Guangwu bestowed on him a seal with a tassel."<ref name="CityMuseum" /><ref>{{Cite book | last = Tsunoda | first = Ryusaku | title = Japan in the Chinese Dynastic Histories: Later Han Through Ming Dynasties | editor-last = Goodrich | editor-first = L. Carrington | publisher = P.D. and Ione Perkins | location = South Pasadena, CA | year = 1951 | pages = 187 | series = Perkins Asiatic monographs }}</ref>
During the Han dynasty, similar seals were bestowed on other regional sovereigns, in an attempt by the dynasty to bring these sovereigns into the Han ruling order.<ref name="CityMuseum" />
=== Rediscovery === [[File:Kin-in Park02.jpg|thumb|260px|Commemorative monument near the site where the seal was uncovered in 1784]] After being lost for an undetermined period of time, the seal was reportedly rediscovered on April 12, 1784, on [[Shika Island]] in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], Japan. According to contemporary reports, the seal was discovered by a farmer named Jinbei while repairing an irrigation ditch. It was found surrounded by stones forming a box-like structure around it. The stone above the seal required two adults to lift. After rediscovery, the seal was kept by the [[:en:Kuroda clan|Kuroda clan]], rulers of the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]], and eventually donated by the Kuroda family to the city of Fukuoka in 1978.<ref name="CityMuseum" />
== See also == * [[:en:King of Dian gold seal|King of Dian gold seal]] * [[:en:King of Nanyue gold seal|King of Nanyue gold seal]] * [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)|List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)]]
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

}}

[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|King of Na gold seal}} [[fr:Sceau du roi de Na]] [[id:Segel emas Raja Na]] [[ja:漢委奴国王印]] [[ko:한위노국왕인]] [[ru:Печать ханского вана японской страны На]] [[simple:King of Na gold seal]] [[uk:Печатка вана японської країни На]] [[zh:汉委奴国王印]]

{{Short description|Chinese gold seal discovered in Japan}} [[File:King of Na gold seal.jpg|thumb|upright=1.15|Composite image showing two views of the seal]] [[File:King of Na gold seal knob top.png|thumb|upright=1.15|Top view of the snake knob]]
The '''King of Na gold seal''' ({{langx|ja|漢委奴国王印}}) is a solid [[:en:Gold|Gold]] [[:en:Seal (East Asia)|seal]] discovered in the year 1784 on [[Shikanoshima Island]] in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The seal is designated as a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book | publisher = [[Yomiuri Shimbun]] | title = National Treasures of Japan – Exhibition catalogue, April 10 - May 27, 1990, Tokyo National Museum | year = 1990 | language = Japanese }}</ref> The seal is believed to have been cast in [[:en:Han dynasty|China]] and bestowed by [[:en:Emperor Guangwu of Han|Emperor Guangwu of Han]] upon a diplomatic official (envoy) visiting from Japan in the year 57 AD. The five [[:en:Kanji|Chinese characters]] appearing on the seal identify it as the seal of the King of Na state of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa (Japan)]], [[:en:Vassal state|Vassal state]] of the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]].<ref>{{Citation | last = Miyake | first = Yonekichi | title = 漢委奴國王印考 (A study of the seal [inscribed] to the King of the state of Na in Wa under the Han dynasty) | journal = Shigaku Zasshi | volume = 3 | issue = 37 |date=December 1892 | pages = 874–81 }}</ref> The seal is currently in the collection of the [[:en:Fukuoka City Museum|Fukuoka City Museum]] in [[:en:Fukuoka|Fukuoka]], Japan. The seal is very well known in Japan, as its taught in Japanese history books to be a cultural asset which ascertains how Japan as a political entity came into being. Its the first known textual record of Japan as a country.<ref name="CityMuseum">{{Cite web | title = The Permanent Exhibitions of the Fukuoka City Museum | publisher = Fukuoka City Museum | accessdate = 2023-05-10 | url = https://museum.city.fukuoka.jp/en/exhibition.html }}</ref>
== Appearance == The seal is composed of gold of 95% purity.<ref>{{Cite web | title = The Gold Seal, "Kan no Wa no Na no Kokuo" | publisher = Fukuoka Art Museum | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.fukuoka-art-museum.jp/english/eb/html/eb01/2011/kin_in/kin_in.html }}</ref> It is made up of a square base, with the seal itself on the bottom face, and a handle on top of the base in the shape of a coiled serpent. It has a mass of {{convert|108.729|g}}. The total height from base to handle is {{convert|2.236|cm}}. The base of the seal averages {{convert|2.347|cm}} on a side. This dimension roughly corresponds to the traditional Chinese standard unit of length of one [[:en:Cun (unit)|cun]], as used in the [[Han dynasty#Eastern Han|Later Han dynasty]] (about {{convert|2.304|cm|disp=sqbr}}).<ref>{{Cite web | title = 金印 |trans-title=Gold Seal | publisher = Fukuoka Prefectural Shakaikyouiku Sougou Center | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.fsg.pref.fukuoka.jp/bunka/contents_detail.asp?table=t27&fields=f1332&id=1307-102&bunrui=kuni_yuke_biju_koko_koku | language = Japanese }}</ref>
=== Characters engraved on the seal === {{multiple image |total_width=260 |footer=Base of seal (left), together with its imprint (right). The imprint is read from right to left, top to bottom. |width1=149|height1=145| image1 = King of Na gold seal face.png |width2=149|height2=145| image2 = King of Na gold seal imprint.svg }}
The five characters engraved on the seal are (in the order in which they are to be read): : 漢委奴國王
The meanings of these characters (in the context of this seal) are: "Han" (referring to the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]] of China), "[[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]" (an ancient name for Japan), "[[:en:Nakoku|Na]]" (an ancient kingdom / state within Japan), "state / country", and "ruler." Altogether, the meaning of the seal inscription is: "(seal of) the King of the Na state of the Wa [vassal?] of the Han dynasty".
The character 委 is a loan for 倭 ([[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]), an instance of the common practice of [[:zh-tw:通假字|loaning characters]] in Classical Chinese.<ref>{{Cite journal |author=黃當時 |year=2011 |title=金印 「漢委奴国王」 の読みと意味について |journal=中国言語文化研究 |number=11 |publisher=佛教大学中国言語文化研究会 |url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/CB/0011/CB00110L001.pdf |access-date=2023-09-18 }}</ref> The characters are engraved in the [[:en:Seal script|Seal script]] style.
== History == The seal has been judged to be the one described in the ''[[:en:Book of the Later Han|Book of the Later Han]]'', a Chinese chronicle of the history of the [[:en:Eastern Han dynasty|Eastern Han dynasty]]. According to the chronicle, the Chinese [[:en:Emperor Guangwu of Han|Emperor Guangwu]] conferred the seal on a diplomatic official visiting from Japan.
=== Contemporary description of conferral === The following is the original Chinese text from the chronicle: :建武中元二年，倭奴國奉貢朝賀，使人自稱大夫，倭國之極南界也。光武賜以印綬。<ref>{{Cite web | title = 後漢書·東夷列傳第七十五 | trans-title = Houhanshu Part 75 | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.xysa.net/a200/h350/03houhanshu/t-115.htm | language = Chinese | archive-date = 2010-03-31 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20100331133322/http://www.xysa.net/a200/h350/03houhanshu/t-115.htm | url-status = dead }}</ref>
This passage can be translated into English as: :"In the 2nd year of the ''jianwu zhongyuan'' reign period [AD 57], the [[:en:Nakoku|Na]] state of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] sent an envoy with tribute. The envoy introduced himself as a high official. The state lies in the [[Kyushu|far south]] of Wa. [Emperor] Guangwu bestowed on him a seal with a tassel."<ref name="CityMuseum" /><ref>{{Cite book | last = Tsunoda | first = Ryusaku | title = Japan in the Chinese Dynastic Histories: Later Han Through Ming Dynasties | editor-last = Goodrich | editor-first = L. Carrington | publisher = P.D. and Ione Perkins | location = South Pasadena, CA | year = 1951 | pages = 187 | series = Perkins Asiatic monographs }}</ref>
During the Han dynasty, similar seals were bestowed on other regional sovereigns, in an attempt by the dynasty to bring these sovereigns into the Han ruling order.<ref name="CityMuseum" />
=== Rediscovery === [[File:Kin-in Park02.jpg|thumb|260px|Commemorative monument near the site where the seal was uncovered in 1784]] After being lost for an undetermined period of time, the seal was reportedly rediscovered on April 12, 1784, on [[Shika Island]] in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], Japan. According to contemporary reports, the seal was discovered by a farmer named Jinbei while repairing an irrigation ditch. It was found surrounded by stones forming a box-like structure around it. The stone above the seal required two adults to lift. After rediscovery, the seal was kept by the [[:en:Kuroda clan|Kuroda clan]], rulers of the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]], and eventually donated by the Kuroda family to the city of Fukuoka in 1978.<ref name="CityMuseum" />
== See also == * [[:en:King of Dian gold seal|King of Dian gold seal]] * [[:en:King of Nanyue gold seal|King of Nanyue gold seal]] * [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)|List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)]]

}}

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q2975212]] Wikidata name: King of Na gold seal [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Chinese gold seal discovered in Japan}} [[File:King of Na gold seal.jpg|thumb|upright=1.15|Composite image showing two views of the seal]] [[File:King of Na gold seal knob top.png|thumb|upright=1.15|Top view of the snake knob]]
The '''King of Na gold seal''' ({{langx|ja|漢委奴国王印}}) is a solid [[:en:Gold|Gold]] [[:en:Seal (East Asia)|seal]] discovered in the year 1784 on [[:en:Shikanoshima Island|Shikanoshima Island]] in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The seal is designated as a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book | publisher = [[Yomiuri Shimbun]] | title = National Treasures of Japan – Exhibition catalogue, April 10 - May 27, 1990, Tokyo National Museum | year = 1990 | language = Japanese }}</ref> The seal is believed to have been cast in [[:en:Han dynasty|China]] and bestowed by [[:en:Emperor Guangwu of Han|Emperor Guangwu of Han]] upon a diplomatic official (envoy) visiting from Japan in the year 57 AD. The five [[:en:Kanji|Chinese characters]] appearing on the seal identify it as the seal of the King of Na state of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa (Japan)]], [[:en:Vassal state|Vassal state]] of the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]].<ref>{{Citation | last = Miyake | first = Yonekichi | title = 漢委奴國王印考 (A study of the seal [inscribed] to the King of the state of Na in Wa under the Han dynasty) | journal = Shigaku Zasshi | volume = 3 | issue = 37 |date=December 1892 | pages = 874–81 }}</ref> The seal is currently in the collection of the [[:en:Fukuoka City Museum|Fukuoka City Museum]] in [[:en:Fukuoka|Fukuoka]], Japan. The seal is very well known in Japan, as its taught in Japanese history books to be a cultural asset which ascertains how Japan as a political entity came into being. Its the first known textual record of Japan as a country.<ref name="CityMuseum">{{Cite web | title = The Permanent Exhibitions of the Fukuoka City Museum | publisher = Fukuoka City Museum | accessdate = 2023-05-10 | url = https://museum.city.fukuoka.jp/en/exhibition.html }}</ref>
== Appearance == The seal is composed of gold of 95% purity.<ref>{{Cite web | title = The Gold Seal, "Kan no Wa no Na no Kokuo" | publisher = Fukuoka Art Museum | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.fukuoka-art-museum.jp/english/eb/html/eb01/2011/kin_in/kin_in.html }}</ref> It is made up of a square base, with the seal itself on the bottom face, and a handle on top of the base in the shape of a coiled serpent. It has a mass of {{convert|108.729|g}}. The total height from base to handle is {{convert|2.236|cm}}. The base of the seal averages {{convert|2.347|cm}} on a side. This dimension roughly corresponds to the traditional Chinese standard unit of length of one [[:en:Cun (unit)|cun]], as used in the [[Han dynasty#Eastern Han|Later Han dynasty]] (about {{convert|2.304|cm|disp=sqbr}}).<ref>{{Cite web | title = 金印 |trans-title=Gold Seal | publisher = Fukuoka Prefectural Shakaikyouiku Sougou Center | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.fsg.pref.fukuoka.jp/bunka/contents_detail.asp?table=t27&fields=f1332&id=1307-102&bunrui=kuni_yuke_biju_koko_koku | language = Japanese }}</ref>
=== Characters engraved on the seal === {{multiple image |total_width=260 |footer=Base of seal (left), together with its imprint (right). The imprint is read from right to left, top to bottom. |width1=149|height1=145| image1 = King of Na gold seal face.png |width2=149|height2=145| image2 = King of Na gold seal imprint.svg }}
The five characters engraved on the seal are (in the order in which they are to be read): : 漢委奴國王
The meanings of these characters (in the context of this seal) are: "Han" (referring to the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]] of China), "[[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]" (an ancient name for Japan), "[[:en:Nakoku|Na]]" (an ancient kingdom / state within Japan), "state / country", and "ruler." Altogether, the meaning of the seal inscription is: "(seal of) the King of the Na state of the Wa [vassal?] of the Han dynasty".
The character 委 is a loan for 倭 ([[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]), an instance of the common practice of [[:zh-tw:通假字|loaning characters]] in Classical Chinese.<ref>{{Cite journal |author=黃當時 |year=2011 |title=金印 「漢委奴国王」 の読みと意味について |journal=中国言語文化研究 |number=11 |publisher=佛教大学中国言語文化研究会 |url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/CB/0011/CB00110L001.pdf |access-date=2023-09-18 }}</ref> The characters are engraved in the [[:en:Seal script|Seal script]] style.
== History == The seal has been judged to be the one described in the ''[[:en:Book of the Later Han|Book of the Later Han]]'', a Chinese chronicle of the history of the [[:en:Eastern Han dynasty|Eastern Han dynasty]]. According to the chronicle, the Chinese [[:en:Emperor Guangwu of Han|Emperor Guangwu]] conferred the seal on a diplomatic official visiting from Japan.
=== Contemporary description of conferral === The following is the original Chinese text from the chronicle: :建武中元二年，倭奴國奉貢朝賀，使人自稱大夫，倭國之極南界也。光武賜以印綬。<ref>{{Cite web | title = 後漢書·東夷列傳第七十五 | trans-title = Houhanshu Part 75 | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.xysa.net/a200/h350/03houhanshu/t-115.htm | language = Chinese | archive-date = 2010-03-31 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20100331133322/http://www.xysa.net/a200/h350/03houhanshu/t-115.htm | url-status = dead }}</ref>
This passage can be translated into English as: :"In the 2nd year of the ''jianwu zhongyuan'' reign period [AD 57], the [[:en:Nakoku|Na]] state of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] sent an envoy with tribute. The envoy introduced himself as a high official. The state lies in the [[Kyushu|far south]] of Wa. [Emperor] Guangwu bestowed on him a seal with a tassel."<ref name="CityMuseum" /><ref>{{Cite book | last = Tsunoda | first = Ryusaku | title = Japan in the Chinese Dynastic Histories: Later Han Through Ming Dynasties | editor-last = Goodrich | editor-first = L. Carrington | publisher = P.D. and Ione Perkins | location = South Pasadena, CA | year = 1951 | pages = 187 | series = Perkins Asiatic monographs }}</ref>
During the Han dynasty, similar seals were bestowed on other regional sovereigns, in an attempt by the dynasty to bring these sovereigns into the Han ruling order.<ref name="CityMuseum" />
=== Rediscovery === [[File:Kin-in Park02.jpg|thumb|260px|Commemorative monument near the site where the seal was uncovered in 1784]] After being lost for an undetermined period of time, the seal was reportedly rediscovered on April 12, 1784, on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]] in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], Japan. According to contemporary reports, the seal was discovered by a farmer named Jinbei while repairing an irrigation ditch. It was found surrounded by stones forming a box-like structure around it. The stone above the seal required two adults to lift. After rediscovery, the seal was kept by the [[:en:Kuroda clan|Kuroda clan]], rulers of the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]], and eventually donated by the Kuroda family to the city of Fukuoka in 1978.<ref name="CityMuseum" />
== See also == * [[:en:King of Dian gold seal|King of Dian gold seal]] * [[:en:King of Nanyue gold seal|King of Nanyue gold seal]] * [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)|List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)]]
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]]" to "[[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:"
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q2975212]] Wikidata name: King of Na gold seal [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q2975212]] Wikidata name: King of Na gold seal [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Chinese gold seal discovered in Japan}} [[File:King of Na gold seal.jpg|thumb|upright=1.15|Composite image showing two views of the seal]] [[File:King of Na gold seal knob top.png|thumb|upright=1.15|Top view of the snake knob]]
The '''King of Na gold seal''' ({{langx|ja|漢委奴国王印}}) is a solid [[:en:Gold|Gold]] [[:en:Seal (East Asia)|seal]] discovered in the year 1784 on [[:en:Shikanoshima Island|Shikanoshima Island]] in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The seal is designated as a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book | publisher = [[Yomiuri Shimbun]] | title = National Treasures of Japan – Exhibition catalogue, April 10 - May 27, 1990, Tokyo National Museum | year = 1990 | language = Japanese }}</ref> The seal is believed to have been cast in [[:en:Han dynasty|China]] and bestowed by [[:en:Emperor Guangwu of Han|Emperor Guangwu of Han]] upon a diplomatic official (envoy) visiting from Japan in the year 57 AD. The five [[:en:Kanji|Chinese characters]] appearing on the seal identify it as the seal of the King of Na state of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa (Japan)]], [[:en:Vassal state|Vassal state]] of the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]].<ref>{{Citation | last = Miyake | first = Yonekichi | title = 漢委奴國王印考 (A study of the seal [inscribed] to the King of the state of Na in Wa under the Han dynasty) | journal = Shigaku Zasshi | volume = 3 | issue = 37 |date=December 1892 | pages = 874–81 }}</ref> The seal is currently in the collection of the [[:en:Fukuoka City Museum|Fukuoka City Museum]] in [[:en:Fukuoka|Fukuoka]], Japan. The seal is very well known in Japan, as its taught in Japanese history books to be a cultural asset which ascertains how Japan as a political entity came into being. Its the first known textual record of Japan as a country.<ref name="CityMuseum">{{Cite web | title = The Permanent Exhibitions of the Fukuoka City Museum | publisher = Fukuoka City Museum | accessdate = 2023-05-10 | url = https://museum.city.fukuoka.jp/en/exhibition.html }}</ref>
== Appearance == The seal is composed of gold of 95% purity.<ref>{{Cite web | title = The Gold Seal, "Kan no Wa no Na no Kokuo" | publisher = Fukuoka Art Museum | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.fukuoka-art-museum.jp/english/eb/html/eb01/2011/kin_in/kin_in.html }}</ref> It is made up of a square base, with the seal itself on the bottom face, and a handle on top of the base in the shape of a coiled serpent. It has a mass of {{convert|108.729|g}}. The total height from base to handle is {{convert|2.236|cm}}. The base of the seal averages {{convert|2.347|cm}} on a side. This dimension roughly corresponds to the traditional Chinese standard unit of length of one [[:en:Cun (unit)|cun]], as used in the [[Han dynasty#Eastern Han|Later Han dynasty]] (about {{convert|2.304|cm|disp=sqbr}}).<ref>{{Cite web | title = 金印 |trans-title=Gold Seal | publisher = Fukuoka Prefectural Shakaikyouiku Sougou Center | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.fsg.pref.fukuoka.jp/bunka/contents_detail.asp?table=t27&fields=f1332&id=1307-102&bunrui=kuni_yuke_biju_koko_koku | language = Japanese }}</ref>
=== Characters engraved on the seal === {{multiple image |total_width=260 |footer=Base of seal (left), together with its imprint (right). The imprint is read from right to left, top to bottom. |width1=149|height1=145| image1 = King of Na gold seal face.png |width2=149|height2=145| image2 = King of Na gold seal imprint.svg }}
The five characters engraved on the seal are (in the order in which they are to be read): : 漢委奴國王
The meanings of these characters (in the context of this seal) are: "Han" (referring to the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]] of China), "[[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]" (an ancient name for Japan), "[[:en:Nakoku|Na]]" (an ancient kingdom / state within Japan), "state / country", and "ruler." Altogether, the meaning of the seal inscription is: "(seal of) the King of the Na state of the Wa [vassal?] of the Han dynasty".
The character 委 is a loan for 倭 ([[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]), an instance of the common practice of [[:zh-tw:通假字|loaning characters]] in Classical Chinese.<ref>{{Cite journal |author=黃當時 |year=2011 |title=金印 「漢委奴国王」 の読みと意味について |journal=中国言語文化研究 |number=11 |publisher=佛教大学中国言語文化研究会 |url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/CB/0011/CB00110L001.pdf |access-date=2023-09-18 }}</ref> The characters are engraved in the [[:en:Seal script|Seal script]] style.
== History == The seal has been judged to be the one described in the ''[[:en:Book of the Later Han|Book of the Later Han]]'', a Chinese chronicle of the history of the [[:en:Eastern Han dynasty|Eastern Han dynasty]]. According to the chronicle, the Chinese [[:en:Emperor Guangwu of Han|Emperor Guangwu]] conferred the seal on a diplomatic official visiting from Japan.
=== Contemporary description of conferral === The following is the original Chinese text from the chronicle: :建武中元二年，倭奴國奉貢朝賀，使人自稱大夫，倭國之極南界也。光武賜以印綬。<ref>{{Cite web | title = 後漢書·東夷列傳第七十五 | trans-title = Houhanshu Part 75 | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.xysa.net/a200/h350/03houhanshu/t-115.htm | language = Chinese | archive-date = 2010-03-31 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20100331133322/http://www.xysa.net/a200/h350/03houhanshu/t-115.htm | url-status = dead }}</ref>
This passage can be translated into English as: :"In the 2nd year of the ''jianwu zhongyuan'' reign period [AD 57], the [[:en:Nakoku|Na]] state of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] sent an envoy with tribute. The envoy introduced himself as a high official. The state lies in the [[Kyushu|far south]] of Wa. [Emperor] Guangwu bestowed on him a seal with a tassel."<ref name="CityMuseum" /><ref>{{Cite book | last = Tsunoda | first = Ryusaku | title = Japan in the Chinese Dynastic Histories: Later Han Through Ming Dynasties | editor-last = Goodrich | editor-first = L. Carrington | publisher = P.D. and Ione Perkins | location = South Pasadena, CA | year = 1951 | pages = 187 | series = Perkins Asiatic monographs }}</ref>
During the Han dynasty, similar seals were bestowed on other regional sovereigns, in an attempt by the dynasty to bring these sovereigns into the Han ruling order.<ref name="CityMuseum" />
=== Rediscovery === [[File:Kin-in Park02.jpg|thumb|260px|Commemorative monument near the site where the seal was uncovered in 1784]] After being lost for an undetermined period of time, the seal was reportedly rediscovered on April 12, 1784, on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]] in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], Japan. According to contemporary reports, the seal was discovered by a farmer named Jinbei while repairing an irrigation ditch. It was found surrounded by stones forming a box-like structure around it. The stone above the seal required two adults to lift. After rediscovery, the seal was kept by the [[:en:Kuroda clan|Kuroda clan]], rulers of the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]], and eventually donated by the Kuroda family to the city of Fukuoka in 1978.<ref name="CityMuseum" />
== See also == * [[:en:King of Dian gold seal|King of Dian gold seal]] * [[:en:King of Nanyue gold seal|King of Nanyue gold seal]] * [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)|List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)]]
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
{{Seal (East Asia)}} {{Authority control}}

[[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Gold objects]] [[Category:1st-century artifacts]] [[Category:1784 in Japan]] [[Category:Archaeological discoveries in Japan]] [[Category:History of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:1784 archaeological discoveries]] [[Category:1st century in Japan]] [[Category:57]]
}}

[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|King of Na gold seal}} [[fr:Sceau du roi de Na]] [[id:Segel emas Raja Na]] [[ja:漢委奴国王印]] [[ko:한위노국왕인]] [[ru:Печать ханского вана японской страны На]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:King of Na gold seal]] [[uk:Печатка вана японської країни На]] [[zh:汉委奴国王印]]

According to contemporary reports, the seal was discovered by a farmer named Jinbei while repairing an irrigation ditch. It was found surrounded by stones forming a box-like structure around it. The stone above the seal required two adults to lift. After rediscovery, the seal was kept by the [[:en:Kuroda clan|Kuroda clan]], rulers of the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]], and eventually donated by the Kuroda family to the city of Fukuoka in 1978.<ref name="CityMuseum" />
== See also == * [[:en:King of Dian gold seal|King of Dian gold seal]] * [[:en:King of Nanyue gold seal|King of Nanyue gold seal]] * [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)|List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)]]

revision.comment: Bot: add interwikis from Wikidata
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:King of Na gold seal]][[en:King of Na gold seal]][[fr:Sceau du roi de Na]][[id:Segel emas Raja Na]][[ja:漢委奴国王印]][[ko:한위노국왕인]][[ru:Печать ханского вана японской страны На]][[simple:King of Na gold seal]][[uk:Печатка вана японської країни На]][[zh:汉委奴国王印]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q2975212]] Wikidata name: King of Na gold seal [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q2975212]] Wikidata name: King of Na gold seal [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Chinese gold seal discovered in Japan}} [[File:King of Na gold seal.jpg|thumb|upright=1.15|Composite image showing two views of the seal]] [[File:King of Na gold seal knob top.png|thumb|upright=1.15|Top view of the snake knob]]
The '''King of Na gold seal''' ({{langx|ja|漢委奴国王印}}) is a solid [[:en:Gold|Gold]] [[:en:Seal (East Asia)|seal]] discovered in the year 1784 on [[:en:Shikanoshima Island|Shikanoshima Island]] in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The seal is designated as a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book | publisher = [[Yomiuri Shimbun]] | title = National Treasures of Japan – Exhibition catalogue, April 10 - May 27, 1990, Tokyo National Museum | year = 1990 | language = Japanese }}</ref> The seal is believed to have been cast in [[:en:Han dynasty|China]] and bestowed by [[:en:Emperor Guangwu of Han|Emperor Guangwu of Han]] upon a diplomatic official (envoy) visiting from Japan in the year 57 AD. The five [[:en:Kanji|Chinese characters]] appearing on the seal identify it as the seal of the King of Na state of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa (Japan)]], [[:en:Vassal state|Vassal state]] of the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]].<ref>{{Citation | last = Miyake | first = Yonekichi | title = 漢委奴國王印考 (A study of the seal [inscribed] to the King of the state of Na in Wa under the Han dynasty) | journal = Shigaku Zasshi | volume = 3 | issue = 37 |date=December 1892 | pages = 874–81 }}</ref> The seal is currently in the collection of the [[:en:Fukuoka City Museum|Fukuoka City Museum]] in [[:en:Fukuoka|Fukuoka]], Japan. The seal is very well known in Japan, as its taught in Japanese history books to be a cultural asset which ascertains how Japan as a political entity came into being. Its the first known textual record of Japan as a country.<ref name="CityMuseum">{{Cite web | title = The Permanent Exhibitions of the Fukuoka City Museum | publisher = Fukuoka City Museum | accessdate = 2023-05-10 | url = https://museum.city.fukuoka.jp/en/exhibition.html }}</ref>
== Appearance == The seal is composed of gold of 95% purity.<ref>{{Cite web | title = The Gold Seal, "Kan no Wa no Na no Kokuo" | publisher = Fukuoka Art Museum | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.fukuoka-art-museum.jp/english/eb/html/eb01/2011/kin_in/kin_in.html }}</ref> It is made up of a square base, with the seal itself on the bottom face, and a handle on top of the base in the shape of a coiled serpent. It has a mass of {{convert|108.729|g}}. The total height from base to handle is {{convert|2.236|cm}}. The base of the seal averages {{convert|2.347|cm}} on a side. This dimension roughly corresponds to the traditional Chinese standard unit of length of one [[:en:Cun (unit)|cun]], as used in the [[Han dynasty#Eastern Han|Later Han dynasty]] (about {{convert|2.304|cm|disp=sqbr}}).<ref>{{Cite web | title = 金印 |trans-title=Gold Seal | publisher = Fukuoka Prefectural Shakaikyouiku Sougou Center | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.fsg.pref.fukuoka.jp/bunka/contents_detail.asp?table=t27&fields=f1332&id=1307-102&bunrui=kuni_yuke_biju_koko_koku | language = Japanese }}</ref>
=== Characters engraved on the seal === {{multiple image |total_width=260 |footer=Base of seal (left), together with its imprint (right). The imprint is read from right to left, top to bottom. |width1=149|height1=145| image1 = King of Na gold seal face.png |width2=149|height2=145| image2 = King of Na gold seal imprint.svg }}
The five characters engraved on the seal are (in the order in which they are to be read): : 漢委奴國王
The meanings of these characters (in the context of this seal) are: "Han" (referring to the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]] of China), "[[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]" (an ancient name for Japan), "[[:en:Nakoku|Na]]" (an ancient kingdom / state within Japan), "state / country", and "ruler." Altogether, the meaning of the seal inscription is: "(seal of) the King of the Na state of the Wa [vassal?] of the Han dynasty".
The character 委 is a loan for 倭 ([[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]), an instance of the common practice of [[:zh-tw:通假字|loaning characters]] in Classical Chinese.<ref>{{Cite journal |author=黃當時 |year=2011 |title=金印 「漢委奴国王」 の読みと意味について |journal=中国言語文化研究 |number=11 |publisher=佛教大学中国言語文化研究会 |url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/CB/0011/CB00110L001.pdf |access-date=2023-09-18 }}</ref> The characters are engraved in the [[:en:Seal script|Seal script]] style.
== History == The seal has been judged to be the one described in the ''[[:en:Book of the Later Han|Book of the Later Han]]'', a Chinese chronicle of the history of the [[:en:Eastern Han dynasty|Eastern Han dynasty]]. According to the chronicle, the Chinese [[:en:Emperor Guangwu of Han|Emperor Guangwu]] conferred the seal on a diplomatic official visiting from Japan.
=== Contemporary description of conferral === The following is the original Chinese text from the chronicle: :建武中元二年，倭奴國奉貢朝賀，使人自稱大夫，倭國之極南界也。光武賜以印綬。<ref>{{Cite web | title = 後漢書·東夷列傳第七十五 | trans-title = Houhanshu Part 75 | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.xysa.net/a200/h350/03houhanshu/t-115.htm | language = Chinese | archive-date = 2010-03-31 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20100331133322/http://www.xysa.net/a200/h350/03houhanshu/t-115.htm | url-status = dead }}</ref>
This passage can be translated into English as: :"In the 2nd year of the ''jianwu zhongyuan'' reign period [AD 57], the [[:en:Nakoku|Na]] state of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] sent an envoy with tribute. The envoy introduced himself as a high official. The state lies in the [[Kyushu|far south]] of Wa. [Emperor] Guangwu bestowed on him a seal with a tassel."<ref name="CityMuseum" /><ref>{{Cite book | last = Tsunoda | first = Ryusaku | title = Japan in the Chinese Dynastic Histories: Later Han Through Ming Dynasties | editor-last = Goodrich | editor-first = L. Carrington | publisher = P.D. and Ione Perkins | location = South Pasadena, CA | year = 1951 | pages = 187 | series = Perkins Asiatic monographs }}</ref>
During the Han dynasty, similar seals were bestowed on other regional sovereigns, in an attempt by the dynasty to bring these sovereigns into the Han ruling order.<ref name="CityMuseum" />
=== Rediscovery === [[File:Kin-in Park02.jpg|thumb|260px|Commemorative monument near the site where the seal was uncovered in 1784]] After being lost for an undetermined period of time, the seal was reportedly rediscovered on April 12, 1784, on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]] in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], Japan. According to contemporary reports, the seal was discovered by a farmer named Jinbei while repairing an irrigation ditch. It was found surrounded by stones forming a box-like structure around it. The stone above the seal required two adults to lift. After rediscovery, the seal was kept by the [[:en:Kuroda clan|Kuroda clan]], rulers of the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]], and eventually donated by the Kuroda family to the city of Fukuoka in 1978.<ref name="CityMuseum" />
== See also == * [[:en:King of Dian gold seal|King of Dian gold seal]] * [[:en:King of Nanyue gold seal|King of Nanyue gold seal]] * [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)|List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)]]

The five characters engraved on the seal are (in the order in which they are to be read): : 漢委奴國王
The meanings of these characters (in the context of this seal) are: "Han" (referring to the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]] of China), "[[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]" (an ancient name for Japan), "[[:en:Nakoku|Na]]" (an ancient kingdom / state within Japan), "state / country", and "ruler." Altogether, the meaning of the seal inscription is: "(seal of) the King of the Na state of the Wa [vassal?] of the Han dynasty".
The character 委 is a loan for 倭 ([[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]), an instance of the common practice of [[:zh-tw:通假字|loaning characters]] in Classical Chinese.<ref>{{Cite journal |author=黃當時 |year=2011 |title=金印 「漢委奴国王」 の読みと意味について |journal=中国言語文化研究 |number=11 |publisher=佛教大学中国言語文化研究会 |url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/CB/0011/CB00110L001.pdf |access-date=2023-09-18 }}</ref> The characters are engraved in the [[:en:Seal script|Seal script]] style.
== History == The seal has been judged to be the one described in the ''[[:en:Book of the Later Han|Book of the Later Han]]'', a Chinese chronicle of the history of the [[:en:Eastern Han dynasty|Eastern Han dynasty]]. According to the chronicle, the Chinese [[:en:Emperor Guangwu of Han|Emperor Guangwu]] conferred the seal on a diplomatic official visiting from Japan.
=== Contemporary description of conferral === The following is the original Chinese text from the chronicle: :建武中元二年，倭奴國奉貢朝賀，使人自稱大夫，倭國之極南界也。光武賜以印綬。<ref>{{Cite web | title = 後漢書·東夷列傳第七十五 | trans-title = Houhanshu Part 75 | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.xysa.net/a200/h350/03houhanshu/t-115.htm | language = Chinese | archive-date = 2010-03-31 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20100331133322/http://www.xysa.net/a200/h350/03houhanshu/t-115.htm | url-status = dead }}</ref>
This passage can be translated into English as: :"In the 2nd year of the ''jianwu zhongyuan'' reign period [AD 57], the [[:en:Nakoku|Na]] state of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] sent an envoy with tribute. The envoy introduced himself as a high official. The state lies in the [[Kyushu|far south]] of Wa. [Emperor] Guangwu bestowed on him a seal with a tassel."<ref name="CityMuseum" /><ref>{{Cite book | last = Tsunoda | first = Ryusaku | title = Japan in the Chinese Dynastic Histories: Later Han Through Ming Dynasties | editor-last = Goodrich | editor-first = L. Carrington | publisher = P.D. and Ione Perkins | location = South Pasadena, CA | year = 1951 | pages = 187 | series = Perkins Asiatic monographs }}</ref>
During the Han dynasty, similar seals were bestowed on other regional sovereigns, in an attempt by the dynasty to bring these sovereigns into the Han ruling order.<ref name="CityMuseum" />
=== Rediscovery === [[File:Kin-in Park02.jpg|thumb|260px|Commemorative monument near the site where the seal was uncovered in 1784]] After being lost for an undetermined period of time, the seal was reportedly rediscovered on April 12, 1784, on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]] in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], Japan. According to contemporary reports, the seal was discovered by a farmer named Jinbei while repairing an irrigation ditch. It was found surrounded by stones forming a box-like structure around it. The stone above the seal required two adults to lift. After rediscovery, the seal was kept by the [[:en:Kuroda clan|Kuroda clan]], rulers of the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]], and eventually donated by the Kuroda family to the city of Fukuoka in 1978.<ref name="CityMuseum" />
== See also == * [[:en:King of Dian gold seal|King of Dian gold seal]] * [[:en:King of Nanyue gold seal|King of Nanyue gold seal]] * [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)|List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:King of Na gold seal]][[en:King of Na gold seal]][[fr:Sceau du roi de Na]][[id:Segel emas Raja Na]][[ja:漢委奴国王印]][[ko:한위노국왕인]][[ru:Печать ханского вана японской страны На]][[simple:King of Na gold seal]][[uk:Печатка вана японської країни На]][[zh:汉委奴国王印]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2975212]] Wikidata name: King of Na gold seal [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2975212]] Wikidata name: King of Na gold seal [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Chinese gold seal discovered in Japan}} [[File:King of Na gold seal.jpg|thumb|upright=1.15|Composite image showing two views of the seal]] [[File:King of Na gold seal knob top.png|thumb|upright=1.15|Top view of the snake knob]]
The '''King of Na gold seal''' ({{langx|ja|漢委奴国王印}}) is a solid [[:en:Gold|Gold]] [[:en:Seal (East Asia)|seal]] discovered in the year 1784 on [[:en:Shikanoshima Island|Shikanoshima Island]] in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The seal is designated as a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book | publisher = [[Yomiuri Shimbun]] | title = National Treasures of Japan – Exhibition catalogue, April 10 - May 27, 1990, Tokyo National Museum | year = 1990 | language = Japanese }}</ref> The seal is believed to have been cast in [[:en:Han dynasty|China]] and bestowed by [[:en:Emperor Guangwu of Han|Emperor Guangwu of Han]] upon a diplomatic official (envoy) visiting from Japan in the year 57 AD. The five [[:en:Kanji|Chinese characters]] appearing on the seal identify it as the seal of the King of Na state of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa (Japan)]], [[:en:Vassal state|Vassal state]] of the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]].<ref>{{Citation | last = Miyake | first = Yonekichi | title = 漢委奴國王印考 (A study of the seal [inscribed] to the King of the state of Na in Wa under the Han dynasty) | journal = Shigaku Zasshi | volume = 3 | issue = 37 |date=December 1892 | pages = 874–81 }}</ref> The seal is currently in the collection of the [[:en:Fukuoka City Museum|Fukuoka City Museum]] in [[:en:Fukuoka|Fukuoka]], Japan. The seal is very well known in Japan, as its taught in Japanese history books to be a cultural asset which ascertains how Japan as a political entity came into being. Its the first known textual record of Japan as a country.<ref name="CityMuseum">{{Cite web | title = The Permanent Exhibitions of the Fukuoka City Museum | publisher = Fukuoka City Museum | accessdate = 2023-05-10 | url = https://museum.city.fukuoka.jp/en/exhibition.html }}</ref>
== Appearance == The seal is composed of gold of 95% purity.<ref>{{Cite web | title = The Gold Seal, "Kan no Wa no Na no Kokuo" | publisher = Fukuoka Art Museum | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.fukuoka-art-museum.jp/english/eb/html/eb01/2011/kin_in/kin_in.html }}</ref> It is made up of a square base, with the seal itself on the bottom face, and a handle on top of the base in the shape of a coiled serpent. It has a mass of {{convert|108.729|g}}. The total height from base to handle is {{convert|2.236|cm}}. The base of the seal averages {{convert|2.347|cm}} on a side. This dimension roughly corresponds to the traditional Chinese standard unit of length of one [[:en:Cun (unit)|cun]], as used in the [[Han dynasty#Eastern Han|Later Han dynasty]] (about {{convert|2.304|cm|disp=sqbr}}).<ref>{{Cite web | title = 金印 |trans-title=Gold Seal | publisher = Fukuoka Prefectural Shakaikyouiku Sougou Center | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.fsg.pref.fukuoka.jp/bunka/contents_detail.asp?table=t27&fields=f1332&id=1307-102&bunrui=kuni_yuke_biju_koko_koku | language = Japanese }}</ref>
=== Characters engraved on the seal === {{multiple image |total_width=260 |footer=Base of seal (left), together with its imprint (right). The imprint is read from right to left, top to bottom. |width1=149|height1=145| image1 = King of Na gold seal face.png |width2=149|height2=145| image2 = King of Na gold seal imprint.svg }}
The five characters engraved on the seal are (in the order in which they are to be read): : 漢委奴國王
The meanings of these characters (in the context of this seal) are: "Han" (referring to the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]] of China), "[[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]" (an ancient name for Japan), "[[:en:Nakoku|Na]]" (an ancient kingdom / state within Japan), "state / country", and "ruler." Altogether, the meaning of the seal inscription is: "(seal of) the King of the Na state of the Wa [vassal?] of the Han dynasty".
The character 委 is a loan for 倭 ([[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]), an instance of the common practice of [[:zh-tw:通假字|loaning characters]] in Classical Chinese.<ref>{{Cite journal |author=黃當時 |year=2011 |title=金印 「漢委奴国王」 の読みと意味について |journal=中国言語文化研究 |number=11 |publisher=佛教大学中国言語文化研究会 |url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/CB/0011/CB00110L001.pdf |access-date=2023-09-18 }}</ref> The characters are engraved in the [[:en:Seal script|Seal script]] style.
== History == The seal has been judged to be the one described in the ''[[:en:Book of the Later Han|Book of the Later Han]]'', a Chinese chronicle of the history of the [[:en:Eastern Han dynasty|Eastern Han dynasty]]. According to the chronicle, the Chinese [[:en:Emperor Guangwu of Han|Emperor Guangwu]] conferred the seal on a diplomatic official visiting from Japan.
=== Contemporary description of conferral === The following is the original Chinese text from the chronicle: :建武中元二年，倭奴國奉貢朝賀，使人自稱大夫，倭國之極南界也。光武賜以印綬。<ref>{{Cite web | title = 後漢書·東夷列傳第七十五 | trans-title = Houhanshu Part 75 | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.xysa.net/a200/h350/03houhanshu/t-115.htm | language = Chinese | archive-date = 2010-03-31 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20100331133322/http://www.xysa.net/a200/h350/03houhanshu/t-115.htm | url-status = dead }}</ref>
This passage can be translated into English as: :"In the 2nd year of the ''jianwu zhongyuan'' reign period [AD 57], the [[:en:Nakoku|Na]] state of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] sent an envoy with tribute. The envoy introduced himself as a high official. The state lies in the [[Kyushu|far south]] of Wa. [Emperor] Guangwu bestowed on him a seal with a tassel."<ref name="CityMuseum" /><ref>{{Cite book | last = Tsunoda | first = Ryusaku | title = Japan in the Chinese Dynastic Histories: Later Han Through Ming Dynasties | editor-last = Goodrich | editor-first = L. Carrington | publisher = P.D. and Ione Perkins | location = South Pasadena, CA | year = 1951 | pages = 187 | series = Perkins Asiatic monographs }}</ref>
During the Han dynasty, similar seals were bestowed on other regional sovereigns, in an attempt by the dynasty to bring these sovereigns into the Han ruling order.<ref name="CityMuseum" />
=== Rediscovery === [[File:Kin-in Park02.jpg|thumb|260px|Commemorative monument near the site where the seal was uncovered in 1784]] After being lost for an undetermined period of time, the seal was reportedly rediscovered on April 12, 1784, on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]] in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], Japan. According to contemporary reports, the seal was discovered by a farmer named Jinbei while repairing an irrigation ditch. It was found surrounded by stones forming a box-like structure around it. The stone above the seal required two adults to lift. After rediscovery, the seal was kept by the [[:en:Kuroda clan|Kuroda clan]], rulers of the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]], and eventually donated by the Kuroda family to the city of Fukuoka in 1978.<ref name="CityMuseum" />

The '''King of Na gold seal''' ({{langx|ja|漢委奴国王印}}) is a solid [[:en:Gold|Gold]] [[:en:Seal (East Asia)|seal]] discovered in the year 1784 on [[:en:Shikanoshima Island|Shikanoshima Island]] in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The seal is designated as a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book | publisher = [[Yomiuri Shimbun]] | title = National Treasures of Japan – Exhibition catalogue, April 10 - May 27, 1990, Tokyo National Museum | year = 1990 | language = Japanese }}</ref> The seal is believed to have been cast in [[:en:Han dynasty|China]] and bestowed by [[:en:Emperor Guangwu of Han|Emperor Guangwu of Han]] upon a diplomatic official (envoy) visiting from Japan in the year 57 AD. The five [[:en:Kanji|Chinese characters]] appearing on the seal identify it as the seal of the King of Na state of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa (Japan)]], [[:en:Vassal state|Vassal state]] of the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]].<ref>{{Citation | last = Miyake | first = Yonekichi | title = 漢委奴國王印考 (A study of the seal [inscribed] to the King of the state of Na in Wa under the Han dynasty) | journal = Shigaku Zasshi | volume = 3 | issue = 37 |date=December 1892 | pages = 874–81 }}</ref> The seal is currently in the collection of the [[:en:Fukuoka City Museum|Fukuoka City Museum]] in [[:en:Fukuoka|Fukuoka]], Japan. The seal is very well known in Japan, as its taught in Japanese history books to be a cultural asset which ascertains how Japan as a political entity came into being. Its the first known textual record of Japan as a country.<ref name="CityMuseum">{{Cite web | title = The Permanent Exhibitions of the Fukuoka City Museum | publisher = Fukuoka City Museum | accessdate = 2023-05-10 | url = https://museum.city.fukuoka.jp/en/exhibition.html }}</ref>
== Appearance == The seal is composed of gold of 95% purity.<ref>{{Cite web | title = The Gold Seal, "Kan no Wa no Na no Kokuo" | publisher = Fukuoka Art Museum | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.fukuoka-art-museum.jp/english/eb/html/eb01/2011/kin_in/kin_in.html }}</ref> It is made up of a square base, with the seal itself on the bottom face, and a handle on top of the base in the shape of a coiled serpent. It has a mass of {{convert|108.729|g}}. The total height from base to handle is {{convert|2.236|cm}}. The base of the seal averages {{convert|2.347|cm}} on a side. This dimension roughly corresponds to the traditional Chinese standard unit of length of one [[:en:Cun (unit)|cun]], as used in the [[Han dynasty#Eastern Han|Later Han dynasty]] (about {{convert|2.304|cm|disp=sqbr}}).<ref>{{Cite web | title = 金印 |trans-title=Gold Seal | publisher = Fukuoka Prefectural Shakaikyouiku Sougou Center | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.fsg.pref.fukuoka.jp/bunka/contents_detail.asp?table=t27&fields=f1332&id=1307-102&bunrui=kuni_yuke_biju_koko_koku | language = Japanese }}</ref>
=== Characters engraved on the seal === {{multiple image |total_width=260 |footer=Base of seal (left), together with its imprint (right). The imprint is read from right to left, top to bottom. |width1=149|height1=145| image1 = King of Na gold seal face.png |width2=149|height2=145| image2 = King of Na gold seal imprint.svg }}
The five characters engraved on the seal are (in the order in which they are to be read): : 漢委奴國王
The meanings of these characters (in the context of this seal) are: "Han" (referring to the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]] of China), "[[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]" (an ancient name for Japan), "[[:en:Nakoku|Na]]" (an ancient kingdom / state within Japan), "state / country", and "ruler." Altogether, the meaning of the seal inscription is: "(seal of) the King of the Na state of the Wa [vassal?] of the Han dynasty".
The character 委 is a loan for 倭 ([[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]), an instance of the common practice of [[:zh-tw:通假字|loaning characters]] in Classical Chinese.<ref>{{Cite journal |author=黃當時 |year=2011 |title=金印 「漢委奴国王」 の読みと意味について |journal=中国言語文化研究 |number=11 |publisher=佛教大学中国言語文化研究会 |url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/CB/0011/CB00110L001.pdf |access-date=2023-09-18 }}</ref> The characters are engraved in the [[:en:Seal script|Seal script]] style.
== History == The seal has been judged to be the one described in the ''[[:en:Book of the Later Han|Book of the Later Han]]'', a Chinese chronicle of the history of the [[:en:Eastern Han dynasty|Eastern Han dynasty]]. According to the chronicle, the Chinese [[:en:Emperor Guangwu of Han|Emperor Guangwu]] conferred the seal on a diplomatic official visiting from Japan.
=== Contemporary description of conferral === The following is the original Chinese text from the chronicle: :建武中元二年，倭奴國奉貢朝賀，使人自稱大夫，倭國之極南界也。光武賜以印綬。<ref>{{Cite web | title = 後漢書·東夷列傳第七十五 | trans-title = Houhanshu Part 75 | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.xysa.net/a200/h350/03houhanshu/t-115.htm | language = Chinese | archive-date = 2010-03-31 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20100331133322/http://www.xysa.net/a200/h350/03houhanshu/t-115.htm | url-status = dead }}</ref>
This passage can be translated into English as: :"In the 2nd year of the ''jianwu zhongyuan'' reign period [AD 57], the [[:en:Nakoku|Na]] state of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] sent an envoy with tribute. The envoy introduced himself as a high official. The state lies in the [[Kyushu|far south]] of Wa. [Emperor] Guangwu bestowed on him a seal with a tassel."<ref name="CityMuseum" /><ref>{{Cite book | last = Tsunoda | first = Ryusaku | title = Japan in the Chinese Dynastic Histories: Later Han Through Ming Dynasties | editor-last = Goodrich | editor-first = L. Carrington | publisher = P.D. and Ione Perkins | location = South Pasadena, CA | year = 1951 | pages = 187 | series = Perkins Asiatic monographs }}</ref>
During the Han dynasty, similar seals were bestowed on other regional sovereigns, in an attempt by the dynasty to bring these sovereigns into the Han ruling order.<ref name="CityMuseum" />
=== Rediscovery === [[File:Kin-in Park02.jpg|thumb|260px|Commemorative monument near the site where the seal was uncovered in 1784]] After being lost for an undetermined period of time, the seal was reportedly rediscovered on April 12, 1784, on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]] in [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], Japan. According to contemporary reports, the seal was discovered by a farmer named Jinbei while repairing an irrigation ditch. It was found surrounded by stones forming a box-like structure around it. The stone above the seal required two adults to lift. After rediscovery, the seal was kept by the [[:en:Kuroda clan|Kuroda clan]], rulers of the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]], and eventually donated by the Kuroda family to the city of Fukuoka in 1978.<ref name="CityMuseum" />
== See also == * [[:en:King of Dian gold seal|King of Dian gold seal]] * [[:en:King of Nanyue gold seal|King of Nanyue gold seal]] * [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)|List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)]]

== See also == * [[:en:King of Dian gold seal|King of Dian gold seal]] * [[:en:King of Nanyue gold seal|King of Nanyue gold seal]] * [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)|List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)]]
