{{Short description|Chinese gold seal discovered in Japan}} [[File:King of Na gold seal.jpg|thumb|upright=1.15|Composite image showing two views of the seal]] [[File:King of Na gold seal knob top.png|thumb|upright=1.15|Top view of the snake knob]]
The '''King of Na gold seal''' ({{langx|ja|漢委奴国王印}}) is a solid {{ill|Gold|en|Gold|qid=Q897|lt=Gold|1=Gold}} [[:en:Seal (East Asia)|seal]] discovered in the year 1784 on [[:en:Shikanoshima Island|Shikanoshima Island]] in {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|qid=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The seal is designated as a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|qid=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure of Japan|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}.<ref>{{Cite book | publisher = [[Yomiuri Shimbun]] | title = National Treasures of Japan – Exhibition catalogue, April 10 - May 27, 1990, Tokyo National Museum | year = 1990 | language = Japanese }}</ref> The seal is believed to have been cast in {{ill|Han dynasty|en|Han dynasty|qid=Q7209|lt=China|1=Han dynasty}} and bestowed by {{ill|Emperor Guangwu of Han|en|Emperor Guangwu of Han|qid=Q7268|lt=Emperor Guangwu of Han|1=Emperor Guangwu of Han}} upon a diplomatic official (envoy) visiting from Japan in the year 57 AD. The five {{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|qid=Q82772|lt=Chinese characters|1=Kanji}} appearing on the seal identify it as the seal of the King of Na state of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa (Japan)]], {{ill|Vassal state|en|Vassal state|qid=Q1371288|lt=Vassal state|1=Vassal state}} of the {{ill|Han dynasty|en|Han dynasty|qid=Q7209|lt=Han dynasty|1=Han dynasty}}.<ref>{{Citation | last = Miyake | first = Yonekichi | title = 漢委奴國王印考 (A study of the seal [inscribed] to the King of the state of Na in Wa under the Han dynasty) | journal = Shigaku Zasshi | volume = 3 | issue = 37 |date=December 1892 | pages = 874–81 }}</ref> The seal is currently in the collection of the {{ill|Fukuoka City Museum|en|Fukuoka City Museum|qid=Q11592224|lt=Fukuoka City Museum|1=Fukuoka City Museum}} in {{ill|Fukuoka|en|Fukuoka|qid=Q26600|lt=Fukuoka|1=Fukuoka}}, Japan. The seal is very well known in Japan, as its taught in Japanese history books to be a cultural asset which ascertains how Japan as a political entity came into being. Its the first known textual record of Japan as a country.<ref name="CityMuseum">{{Cite web | title = The Permanent Exhibitions of the Fukuoka City Museum | publisher = Fukuoka City Museum | accessdate = 2023-05-10 | url = https://museum.city.fukuoka.jp/en/exhibition.html }}</ref>
== Appearance == The seal is composed of gold of 95% purity.<ref>{{Cite web | title = The Gold Seal, "Kan no Wa no Na no Kokuo" | publisher = Fukuoka Art Museum | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.fukuoka-art-museum.jp/english/eb/html/eb01/2011/kin_in/kin_in.html }}</ref> It is made up of a square base, with the seal itself on the bottom face, and a handle on top of the base in the shape of a coiled serpent. It has a mass of {{convert|108.729|g}}. The total height from base to handle is {{convert|2.236|cm}}. The base of the seal averages {{convert|2.347|cm}} on a side. This dimension roughly corresponds to the traditional Chinese standard unit of length of one {{ill|Cun (unit)|en|Cun (unit)|qid=Q876237|lt=cun|1=Cun (unit)}}, as used in the [[Han dynasty#Eastern Han|Later Han dynasty]] (about {{convert|2.304|cm|disp=sqbr}}).<ref>{{Cite web | title = 金印 |trans-title=Gold Seal | publisher = Fukuoka Prefectural Shakaikyouiku Sougou Center | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.fsg.pref.fukuoka.jp/bunka/contents_detail.asp?table=t27&fields=f1332&id=1307-102&bunrui=kuni_yuke_biju_koko_koku | language = Japanese }}</ref>
=== Characters engraved on the seal === {{multiple image |total_width=260 |footer=Base of seal (left), together with its imprint (right). The imprint is read from right to left, top to bottom. |width1=149|height1=145| image1 = King of Na gold seal face.png |width2=149|height2=145| image2 = King of Na gold seal imprint.svg }}
The five characters engraved on the seal are (in the order in which they are to be read): : 漢委奴國王
The meanings of these characters (in the context of this seal) are: "Han" (referring to the {{ill|Han dynasty|en|Han dynasty|qid=Q7209|lt=Han dynasty|1=Han dynasty}} of China), "[[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]" (an ancient name for Japan), "{{ill|Nakoku|en|Nakoku|qid=Q600473|lt=Na|1=Nakoku}}" (an ancient kingdom / state within Japan), "state / country", and "ruler." Altogether, the meaning of the seal inscription is: "(seal of) the King of the Na state of the Wa [vassal?] of the Han dynasty".
The character 委 is a loan for 倭 ([[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]), an instance of the common practice of [[:zh-tw:通假字|loaning characters]] in Classical Chinese.<ref>{{Cite journal |author=黃當時 |year=2011 |title=金印 「漢委奴国王」 の読みと意味について |journal=中国言語文化研究 |number=11 |publisher=佛教大学中国言語文化研究会 |url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/CB/0011/CB00110L001.pdf |access-date=2023-09-18 }}</ref> The characters are engraved in the {{ill|Seal script|en|Seal script|qid=Q1208842|lt=Seal script|1=Seal script}} style.
== History == The seal has been judged to be the one described in the ''{{ill|Book of the Later Han|en|Book of the Later Han|qid=Q430428|lt=Book of the Later Han|1=Book of the Later Han}}'', a Chinese chronicle of the history of the [[:en:Eastern Han dynasty|Eastern Han dynasty]]. According to the chronicle, the Chinese {{ill|Emperor Guangwu of Han|en|Emperor Guangwu of Han|qid=Q7268|lt=Emperor Guangwu|1=Emperor Guangwu of Han}} conferred the seal on a diplomatic official visiting from Japan.
=== Contemporary description of conferral === The following is the original Chinese text from the chronicle: :建武中元二年，倭奴國奉貢朝賀，使人自稱大夫，倭國之極南界也。光武賜以印綬。<ref>{{Cite web | title = 後漢書·東夷列傳第七十五 | trans-title = Houhanshu Part 75 | accessdate = 2011-12-24 | url = http://www.xysa.net/a200/h350/03houhanshu/t-115.htm | language = Chinese | archive-date = 2010-03-31 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20100331133322/http://www.xysa.net/a200/h350/03houhanshu/t-115.htm | url-status = dead }}</ref>
This passage can be translated into English as: :"In the 2nd year of the ''jianwu zhongyuan'' reign period [AD 57], the {{ill|Nakoku|en|Nakoku|qid=Q600473|lt=Na|1=Nakoku}} state of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]] sent an envoy with tribute. The envoy introduced himself as a high official. The state lies in the [[Kyushu|far south]] of Wa. [Emperor] Guangwu bestowed on him a seal with a tassel."<ref name="CityMuseum" /><ref>{{Cite book | last = Tsunoda | first = Ryusaku | title = Japan in the Chinese Dynastic Histories: Later Han Through Ming Dynasties | editor-last = Goodrich | editor-first = L. Carrington | publisher = P.D. and Ione Perkins | location = South Pasadena, CA | year = 1951 | pages = 187 | series = Perkins Asiatic monographs }}</ref>
During the Han dynasty, similar seals were bestowed on other regional sovereigns, in an attempt by the dynasty to bring these sovereigns into the Han ruling order.<ref name="CityMuseum" />
=== Rediscovery === [[File:Kin-in Park02.jpg|thumb|260px|Commemorative monument near the site where the seal was uncovered in 1784]] After being lost for an undetermined period of time, the seal was reportedly rediscovered on April 12, 1784, on {{ill|Shika Island|en|Shika Island|qid=Q3077260|lt=Shika Island|1=Shika Island}} in {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|qid=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}, Japan. According to contemporary reports, the seal was discovered by a farmer named Jinbei while repairing an irrigation ditch. It was found surrounded by stones forming a box-like structure around it. The stone above the seal required two adults to lift. After rediscovery, the seal was kept by the {{ill|Kuroda clan|en|Kuroda clan|qid=Q2975305|lt=Kuroda clan|1=Kuroda clan}}, rulers of the {{ill|Fukuoka Domain|en|Fukuoka Domain|qid=Q208766|lt=Fukuoka Domain|1=Fukuoka Domain}}, and eventually donated by the Kuroda family to the city of Fukuoka in 1978.<ref name="CityMuseum" />
